text.content: DRAFT: LinkedIn Article — How We Built AI Email Automation at Brinc
DRAFT — written by Molty overnight 2026-03-01. Needs Guillermo review + screenshots + personal voice polish before publishing.
We Stopped Spending Hours on Email. Here's What We Built Instead.

Managing partner life means a relentless inbox. Portfolio updates, LP communications, deal flow, partner requests — all demanding attention at the same time, all formatted differently, all urgent. A few months ago I started working with an AI system to handle the parts of email that drain the most time: triaging, summarising, and surfacing what actually matters. What started as an experiment became something we now rely on daily.
The issue was never volume — it was signal vs. noise. We wanted a daily digest of what matters, automatic triage by priority, and zero context-switching to determine if something needs action.

The system pulls from Gmail, categorises threads, summarises long chains, and delivers a clean HTML digest at 6am. Think of it as a daily briefing from your own EA. The HTML format matters: high-priority threads at the top, clear action items, direct links to threads. It reads like a newspaper, not a data dump.
[Screenshot: Example HTML digest — add before publishing]
Gmail API + Claude (Anthropic) for summarisation + Python for orchestration and HTML rendering + Telegram for delivery + cron server running daily at 6am HKT

I check email less, and miss less. The digest ensures important items always surface. The bigger change is cognitive: starting the day with a structured briefing instead of an unstructured inbox is a fundamentally different way to work.
Automating replies — bad idea. Human judgment on tone is irreplaceable. We also overbuilt v1 with too many categories. Three buckets is right: needs action, FYI, skip.

Extending to deal flow and portfolio reporting. Same digest approach to what used to be a manual weekly review.

---
Happy to share more about the architecture. If you've built something similar, what worked?

1. Add 2-3 screenshots of the actual HTML digest
2. Personalise opening with a specific memory that triggered building this
3. Verify tech stack matches exactly
4. Add a metric if you have one (time saved, etc.)
5. Tune to your LinkedIn voice — this is professional-casual
6. Approx 600 words — right length for LinkedIn article
